food_subgroup: Balinese mixed rice with multiple side dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Nasi Campur Bali. Closest parent is a rice-based mixed plate with multiple Balinese small side dishes. No exact FoodOn URI available as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Balinese mixed rice with multiple small side dishes (nasi campur bali style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00PB used as closest parent for composite rice dish with multiple side dishes; no exact Nasi Campur Bali code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Nasi Campur Bali as of 2026.

main_ingredients: satay lilit (minced chicken on lemongrass skewers)
sambal matah (raw chilli, lemongrass, shallots, lime juice)
allergens: gluten-free (no wheat-based ingredients in standard preparation)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Nasi Campur Bali weighs approximately 400g, comprising a mound of steamed white rice surrounded by small portions of chicken satay lilit, lawar, crispy tempeh, sambal matah, and peanuts.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Campur Bali (halal chicken version) — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table; component-based estimation
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single FAO/INFOODS SEA entry exists for Nasi Campur Bali halal chicken version. Values estimated from individual component entries in the FAO/INFOODS SEA table: steamed white rice, grilled chicken, tempeh, green beans, grated coconut, sambal matah ingredients. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used as secondary cross-check for individual components (rice, chicken, tempeh). Values represent a weighted composite for a typical 400g plate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — University of Sydney GI Database; white steamed rice GI ~73 moderated by protein and fat from side dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Nasi Campur Bali. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for white steamed rice (GI ~73). The diverse protein (chicken, tempeh) and fat (coconut in lawar, peanuts) components of the side dishes are well-established to lower the effective GI of a mixed rice meal. GL per 400g serving calculated as: (65 × 84g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 54.6. This is consistent with published mixed-meal GI attenuation literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 295mg/100g; a 400g plate delivers approximately 1,180mg sodium (49% of the 2,400mg daily limit)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250g and request reduced-salt preparation; the sambal matah component is naturally low in sodium and can be consumed freely, but soy-marinated chicken and lawar seasoning are the primary sodium contributors.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~54.6 for a 400g plate) driven by white steamed rice (~84g available carbohydrate per plate)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit rice to 100g per serving (reducing GL to approximately 13.7), increase the proportion of tempeh and chicken side dishes for protein and fibre, and note that sambal matah capsaicin may modestly support postprandial insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.2g/100g (primarily from coconut in lawar component); cholesterol 45mg/100g; a 400g plate delivers 12.8g saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to 200–250g, choose grilled chicken satay lilit over fried components, and note that tempeh isoflavones and the absence of trans fat (0.0g/100g) are favourable; sodium intake from a full plate approaches 50% of the daily limit and should be factored into total daily sodium budget.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lower saturated fat than coconut-milk-heavy dishes; vitamin C from sambal matah (8.5mg/100g); tempeh isoflavones
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Nasi Campur Bali is generally suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; tempeh provides anti-inflammatory isoflavones, sambal matah provides vitamin C supporting collagen synthesis, and the absence of high saturated fat or trans fat reduces pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Campur Bali is the Balinese version of a mixed rice plate — steamed white rice served at the centre of a plate and ringed with a diverse array of small portions of Balinese side dishes. The traditional version features babi guling (roasted suckling pig), lawar (minced meat and green beans with grated coconut and spices), satay lilit (minced fish or meat grilled on lemongrass skewers), crispy fried tempeh, and the distinctive sambal matah — a raw fresh Balinese chilli relish made with thinly sliced lemongrass, shallots, kaffir lime leaves, and lime juice. In the halal chicken version, all pork components are replaced with chicken: chicken satay lilit, chicken lawar, and grilled or fried chicken pieces. The dish is a quintessential Balinese food experience celebrated for its diversity of flavours and textures — crispy tempeh, smoky grilled satay, the bright acidic heat of raw sambal matah, and the earthy richness of spiced lawar. Nutritionally, a 400g plate delivers approximately 740 kcal, 36.8g protein, 34g fat, and 84g carbohydrate. The glycemic load is high (~54.6 per plate) due to white rice, making portion control important for diabetic diners. Sodium is moderate at 295mg/100g. Tempeh contributes soy isoflavones and dietary fibre; sambal matah contributes vitamin C and capsaicin. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free in its standard preparation.
common_user_queries: nasi campur bali vs nasi padang

lawar: Balinese minced meat and coconut salad
green bean coconut Bali side dish

confidence_reason: Nutrient values estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component entries for individual ingredients (steamed white rice, grilled chicken, tempeh, green beans, coconut, sambal matah components) cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No single approved-source entry exists for Nasi Campur Bali halal chicken version as a composite dish. GI estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database white rice entry with documented mixed-meal attenuation. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Nasi Campur Bali as a composite dish are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no hydrogenated fats are used in standard Nasi Campur Bali preparation; confirmed consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA component data. All core macros (protein_g 9.2, fat_g 8.5, carbohydrate_g 21.0) are non-zero and represent a composite mixed rice and side-dish plate. FoodEx2 code A00PB used as closest parent; no exact Nasi Campur Bali code exists. FoodOn URI is null as no specific FoodOn concept exists for this dish. SNOMED CT code is null as no specific concept exists. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white steamed rice GI ~73 from University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by protein and fat from side dishes; GL of 54.6 calculated for a 400g standard plate. The input description was truncated; the full description has been reconstructed from culinary knowledge and the llm_context field of the input record.
